## Slides: Data formats

## Slides: What each file remembers

| Format | Main evidence |
|---|---|
| FASTA / FASTQ | Sequences; FASTQ also stores qualities |
| PAF | Pairwise sequence mappings, including WFMASH output |
| BAM / CRAM | Linear or surjected alignments |
| VCF / gVCF | Variants, genotypes, and reference-confidence blocks |
| BED / GFF / GTF | Intervals and annotations on coordinates |

## Slides: Pangenome files used in this course

| Format | Role |
|---|---|
| GFA | Portable pangenome graph interchange |
| ODGI `.og` | ODGI working graph |
| rGFA | Reference-anchored graph |
| GAF / GAM | Graph alignments |
| GBZ | Compressed graph plus haplotype paths |
| XG / GBWT | vg graph and haplotype indexes |

> GFA is to pangenome graphs what FASTA is to linear sequences.

## Slides: From assemblies to course files

```text
FASTA assemblies
      |
      v
   WFMASH --------> PAF
      |
      v
    PGGB ---------> GFA
                     |
          +----------+----------+
          v                     v
       ODGI .og          VCF / GAF / GBZ
```

## Slides: Raw data still matters

- Illumina BCL/intensity data precedes FASTQ
- Nanopore POD5/FAST5 preserves signal
- Basecalling and filtering discard information
- Reprocessing requires chemistry, software, and metadata

> A file format is a biological contract.

## Slides: FASTQ files are not equivalent

A FASTQ may represent:

- Short-read sequencing-by-synthesis
- HiFi DNA
- Nanopore DNA or direct RNA
- Bulk RNA-seq
- Single-cell libraries

The same syntax does not imply the same experiment.
